aliases: Wonton noodles dry style with shrimp dumplings

english: Wonton noodles dry style with shrimp dumplings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for shrimp dumpling dry noodles; broader 'noodle dish' term used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Singaporean shrimp dumpling dry noodles; A0FJP (noodle-based dish) used as the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept identified for this specific dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of shrimp dumpling dry noodles in Singapore typically weighs approximately 250 g, comprising a portion of egg noodles tossed in gravy sauce with three to four boiled shrimp dumplings on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp Dumpling Noodles (Dry) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Shrimp Dumpling Noodles (Dry), CR ID F05060116. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Egg noodles, cooked — GI value 58 (analogous estimate for egg noodle-based dish)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean shrimp dumpling dry noodles. GI of 58 (medium) is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked egg noodles, which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (58) × carbohydrate per serving (15.18 g/100g × 250 g serving = 37.95 g) / 100 = 22.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 525 mg/100 g; a standard 250 g serving delivers approximately 1,313 mg sodium — exceeding half the AHA daily limit of 2,300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g, request reduced oyster sauce and soy sauce preparation, and avoid adding condiments at the table to keep sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate at 15.18 g/100 g with a medium GI of 58; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 38 g of carbohydrates with very low dietary fibre (0.4 g/100 g) to moderate the glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit serving size to approximately 150–180 g, pair with non-starchy vegetables such as choy sum or bok choy to increase fibre, and monitor total carbohydrate intake per meal to avoid postprandial glucose spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 525 mg/100 g and saturated fat estimated at 1.8 g/100 g from lard-based tossing sauce; combined with low dietary fibre (0.4 g/100 g), this dish presents a moderate cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request lard-free preparation using sesame oil instead, reduce portion size, and supplement the meal with fibre-rich vegetables to offset the sodium and saturated fat load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shrimp Dumpling Noodles (Dry), known in Chinese as 虾饺捞面 (Har Gao Lou Mian), is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of springy egg noodles tossed dry in a savoury gravy sauce made from oyster sauce, soy sauce, and lard or shallot oil, served alongside boiled shrimp dumplings (har gao) filled with whole or minced shrimp. Unlike the soup version, the dry preparation coats the noodles in a glossy, umami-rich sauce that clings to each strand, delivering a more concentrated flavour profile. The dish is commonly found at hawker centres and coffee shops across Singapore and is typically consumed as a breakfast or lunch meal. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 137 kcal, 6.25 g protein, 5.75 g fat, and 15.18 g carbohydrates, with sodium at 525 mg — placing it in the borderline-to-high sodium category. Dietary fibre is very low at 0.4 g/100 g. The glycaemic index is estimated at 58 (medium) based on the egg noodle component. Clinically, the dish is of moderate concern for type 2 diabetes due to its medium-GI carbohydrate load and low fibre, and of high concern for hypertension due to its elevated sodium content driven by oyster sauce and soy sauce. Cardiovascular risk is moderate, primarily from sodium and saturated fat in the lard-based sauce. Consumers are advised to request reduced-sauce preparation and add vegetables to improve the nutritional profile.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05060116) at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using cooked egg noodles as the closest approved-source equivalent, documented accordingly. Saturated fat estimated from typical lard-based sauce composition as HPB does not report this sub-fraction; documented in notes.
notes: sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source (F05060116) — the dish contains no added sugar and the noodle and dumpling components contribute negligible free sugars. saturated_fat_g of 1.8 g/100 g is estimated from typical lard and shallot oil composition used in dry noodle tossing sauce, as HPB does not report saturated fat sub-fractions for this entry; this value should be verified against a detailed fatty acid analysis. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific Singaporean hawker preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry F05060116 and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this composite dish as of 2026. The cultural classification in the input JSON incorrectly listed primary_cuisine as 'western'; this has been corrected to 'Singaporean Chinese' based on the dish's established culinary origin. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (cooked egg noodles) as no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in any approved source. Glycemic load per serving calculated at 22.0 g based on 250 g serving size and 15.18 g/100 g carbohydrate content.
